version_history.subject: [PATCH] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks from unconditional guard destructors in the Linux kernel. The guards always acquire a lock, so the pointer can never be NULL. This change improves performance by reducing code complexity and eliminating unnecessary checks.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] mm: add NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY node page counter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new per-node page counter called NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY to track the number of dirty pages that have been dirtied via RWF_DONTCACHE writes, also known as dropbehind writes. The counter is incremented when a folio is dirtied with the dropbehind flag set and decremented when the flag is cleared or the folio is cleaned. This information will be visible in /proc/vmstat and used by the writeback flusher to determine how many pages to write back during expedited writeback for IOCB_DONTCACHE writes, without flushing all dirty pages.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses performance issues in the IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path by moving writeback submission off the writer's hot path and into a background flusher. The patch introduces a new WB_start_dontcache bit that uses targeted dirty tracking to limit the scope of expedited writeback, preserving I/O batching while reducing latency. The patch also adds a new writeback reason for tracing visibility and targets the correct cgroup writeback domain. Performance results show significant improvements in sequential write throughput and latency, as well as random write throughput and latency.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Andrew Morton asked naive questions about the patch's implementation, specifically whether the WB_start_dontcache bit is properly cleared when a writeback flusher is kicked and whether the wb_check_start_dontcache() handler correctly handles the case where there are no dirty pages to write back.

Reviewer Andrew Morton questioned whether memory is properly cleaned up after generic_write_sync() returns, ensuring it's not still dirty or reclaimable.

The reviewer questioned whether the patch's change would prevent a buildup of dirty, unreclaimable pagecache, and asked if there is any mechanism to limit this buildup.
sentiment_signals: lack of clear objection or suggestion
review_comments.raw_body:
Naive questions...

On Sun, 26 Apr 2026 07:56:08 -0400 Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:

---

So in the current case, when generic_write_sync() returns, all that
memory is written back and clean&reclaimable (or freed?), yes?

---

Whereas after this change, that pagecache is probably still dirty,
unreclaimable, waiting for the flusher to do its thing?

So is there potential that the system will get all gummed up with
dirty, to-be-written-soon pagecache?  Is there something which limits
this buildup?

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Andrew Morton's concern about the IO submission timing in the writeback path, explaining that the current implementation submits I/Os before returning but doesn't wait for completion.

The author acknowledges that the current DONTCACHE I/Os do not wait for completion, but notes that this patch defers I/O submission to a flusher thread, which should eventually take care of business. The author does not indicate a need for further revision.

The author addressed Andrew Morton's concern about the patch potentially causing writers to be limited by traditional VM limits instead of the backing device throughput, explaining that this is actually a desired outcome and that the peak pagecache usage was higher but still less than with normal buffered I/O. The author suggests considering going back to submitting writes inline when the number of dirty pages is high, but notes that this could have negative performance effects.
review_comments.raw_body:
No. Before returning, it submits the I/Os for the portion that it wrote
rather than leaving it to the flusher to take care of things, but it
doesn't wait for the I/Os to complete.

---

Correct, but that's sort of the case today too since DONTCACHE I/Os
don't wait for the completion. With this change we're just deferring
the I/O submission to the flusher thread (which should hopefully soon
wake and take care of business). If the flusher thread can't keep up,
then eventually balance_dirty_pages() will kick in and start slowing
things down.

---

Today in this situation, the writers are limited by the backing device
throughput. Once the I/O submission queues are full, then the DONTCACHE
writers end up stacking up on those. With this change, the writers will
be more limited by traditional VM limits in this situation. 

In the test runs I did, the peak pagecache with DONTCACHE writes was
higher than with the unpatched version but still considerably less than
with normal buffered I/O. That's the cost of deferring the I/O
submission to the flusher.

One thing we could consider is going back to submitting the writes
inline when the number of dirty pages is high. But, that could have a
detrimental effect on performance too.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that the mixed-mode benchmark results show decreased read throughput due to increased write throughput, and suggests there are multiple possible solutions to address this issue.

The author acknowledges that the patch introduces tradeoffs, but believes it's a win overall due to DONTCACHE being relatively new and having a small blast radius. They suggest considering kicking off writeback earlier in the normal buffered case as well.
review_comments.raw_body:
Forgot to comment on this part earlier...

This is the "mixed-mode" (dontcache writes + buffered reads). I played
with a bunch of different settings under nfsd, and those settings
turned out to perform the best with this benchmark.

I suspect what's happening is that the increase in write throughput
from writing via the flusher thread is crowding out reads. So, read
throughput suffers in this test from that. There are a number of ways
we could probably make that more fair.

---

I think it's a win overall. As with anything writeback-related, it's a
game of tradeoffs. The good news is that DONTCACHE is still fairly new
and not many applications are using it yet, so the blast radius from
any change here should be rather small.

As a side note: I've long thought that we in general wait too long to
kick off writeback with normal buffered I/O, particularly with modern
memory sizes. DONTCACHE gives us a place to experiment with this
scheme, but we may want to think about kicking off writeback earlier in
the normal buffered case too.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Matthew Wilcox expressed surprise that the patch's solution for 'noisy neighbour' problem doesn't address his initial expectation of concurrent DONTCACHE and buffered writes, suggesting a different scenario than what the patch aims to solve.
review_comments.raw_body:
hm.  This wasn't what I thought of when I thought of "noisy neighbour".
I'd have process A doing DONTCACHE writes to file A and process B doing
normal buffered writes to file B.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the writeback flusher writes back all dirty pages, regardless of type, and suggested documenting this change in the commit message or man page to clarify the behavior for RWF_DONTCACHE writes.

Reviewer requested testing of parallel buffered and don't cache writes, noting that the patch series impacts this scenario.

Reviewer Ritesh (IBM) requested clarification on test scenario details, specifically pointing out inconsistencies in file size used for single-client tests versus multi-client tests, and suggested using fio's built-in aggregation capabilities instead of parsing results manually.

Reviewer Ritesh requested an explanation for the significant performance improvement (5x) observed on NFS compared to local filesystems, indicating that he is not familiar with the NFS side and would appreciate a clarification.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yup, so, we wakeup the writeback flusher, which will write those many
"number" of dirty pages. Those dirty pages written by writeback, can be
of any type though, can be DONTCACHE or normal (non-dontcache) dirty
pages. IIUC, writeback doesn't distinguish between them while writing.


IMO, what we could also include in the commit msg is why is this above
approach taken? IIUC, that is because, by writing NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY
pages, it still reduces the page cache pressure and still reduces the
amount of work that the reclaim has to do, even though some of those
pages maybe non-dontcache pages, in case if there was a parallel
buffered write in the system.


Also should the following change be documented somewhere? Like in Man
page maybe? i.e.
Earlier RWF_DONTCACHE writes made sure that those dirty pages are
immediately submitted for writeback and completion would release those
pages. But now, in certain cases when there is a mixed buffered write in
the system, those dontcache dirty pages might be written back after a
delay (whenever the next time writeback kicks in).
However for RWF_DONTCACHE reads, it should not affect anything.

---

Can we please also test parallel buffered writes and dontcache writes? 
Since this patch series definitely affects that.

BTW - adding these numbers in the commit msg itself is much helpful.

---

Can you please help describe this test scenario if possible.. In above
you mentioned we are writing file_size as 2x RAM_SIZE. But your
multi-client tests says something else..

local num_clients=4
+	mem_kb=$(awk '/MemTotal/ {print $2}' /proc/meminfo)
+	client_size="$(( mem_kb / 1024 / num_clients ))M"

Also the multi-writer case is spawning parallel fio jobs, and then
parsing and aggregating the bandwidth results instead of using fio to
spawn multiple parallel threads... which is ok, but a bit wierd.
Why not let fio do the aggregate bandwidth, and latency calculation
instead?

---

Nice :)
Some explaination here of why 5x improvement with NFS compared to local
filesystems please?
(I am not much aware of NFS side, but a possible reasoning would help)

-ritesh

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author confirms that the feedback from Jan and HCH about using a flusher kick is being addressed, as it was their suggested approach.

The author is addressing a concern about the documentation of IOCB_DONTCACHE in the preadv/writev manpage, stating that the current description accurately reflects the behavior and does not need to be updated after this change.

Author clarified that the patch only affects DONTCACHE writes, not normal buffered writes, and asked for clarification on what specific test is being requested.

The author acknowledged that the fio testsuite needs to be reworked and agreed to take a closer look at it, indicating a fix is planned.

The author is addressing a concern about the patch's impact on NFS writes, suggesting that the scattered nature of these writes causes performance issues due to competing threads submitting jobs to the queue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Correct. This was the approach that Jan and HCH suggested in the
responses to the last posting.

---

Looks like DONTCACHE is documented in the preadv/writev manpage. Here's
the current blurb about writes:

    Additionally, any range dirtied by a write operation with RWF_DONT‐
    CACHE  set  will  get kicked off for writeback.  This is similar to
    calling  sync_file_range(2)  with  SYNC_FILE_RANGE_WRITE  to  start
    writeback on the given range.  RWF_DONTCACHE is a hint, or best ef‐
    fort,  where  no hard guarantees are given on the state of the page
    cache once the operation completes.

I don't think this verbiage is invalid after this change. Kicking off
writeback is still just a hint, like it was before. We could mention
about how that I/O can compete with regular buffered I/O, but it seems
a bit like we're adding info that will just be confusing for users.

---

To be clear, this only affects DONTCACHE, not normal buffered writes,
but I guess you're referring to the fact that DONTCACHE and buffered
writes can compete now.

Can you clarify specifically what you'd like me to test here? Are you
saying you want me to test parallel and buffered writes together at the
same time (i.e. make them compete?).

I should be able to do that for the local benchmarks, but nfsd's iomode
settings are global and that won't be possible there.

---

That's what I get for asking Claude to roll a testsuite. I'm not that
well-versed in fio, but that makes sense. I'll have a look at reworking
it along those lines.

---

I suspect that it's because of the "scattered" nature of nfsd writes.
When the client sends a write to nfsd, we wake a nfsd thread to service
it. So, if there are a lot of writes operating in parallel, they all
get done in the context of different tasks.

My hunch is that this I/O pattern (writing to same file from a bunch of
different threads), particularly suffers from the DONTCACHE inline
write behavior. The threads all end up competing to submit jobs to the
queue and that causes the performance to fall off sharply.

Thanks for the review!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing Matthew Wilcox's concern about the patch's benchmarking methodology, specifically that it was only tested on NFS and not a local file system. The author explains that they initially tested both reads and writes to the same setting but later experimented with different modes for better performance.
review_comments.raw_body:
Originally, I was benchmarking this via nfsd and only later did I add

So initially, I had it testing them with both reads and writes set to

Thanks,
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jan Kara noted that using a per-node stat (NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY) to determine the amount of dirty folios for writeback may not accurately reflect the actual bandwidth, especially when different cgroups have varying amounts of DONTCACHE writes and normal writes. He suggested changing the stat to be per-bdi_writeback instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
So this will end up splitting NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY folios among per-cgroup wb
structures based on their writeback bandwidth. This is a reasonable thing
for global writeback where the bandwidth more or less corresponds to the
amount of dirty folios. However with DONTCACHE I expect big differences in
among NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY among different cgroups not necessarily
corresponding to wb throughput. In particular if you do DONTCACHE writes
from one cgroup and normal writes from another cgroup this will
systematically underestimate the amount needed to write by a factor of
about two.

So I think the stat should be a per bdi_writeback one (instead of a per node
one) which would avoid the need to split the value to wb here.

								Honza


-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that dontcache marked pages can be delayed from being evicted from the page cache when there is a parallel buffered writer dirtying other page cache pages, potentially impacting performance in certain workloads.

Reviewer Ritesh pointed out that the patch's performance benefits are not evident in baseline cases, and suggested evaluating scenarios where buffered writers dirty page cache pages, which can overflow RAM size, to see if dontcache shines in those situations.

Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where the WB_start_dontcache bit is already set, which could lead to unexpected behavior and requested a check for this condition.
review_comments.raw_body:
The reason I am thinking of this is: dontcache marked pages, gets
evicted from page cache after they are written back. But this patch
series can now delay that from happening when there is a parallel
buffered writer dirtying page cache pages. Because of the reasons we
already discussed...

Note that, this may not be a workload which matters in the real world,
but I was thinking, it will be good to know the impact if any, of such
workload with this patch series (parallel buffered and dontcache
writers).

---

... If we see the performace of buffered and dontcache in baseline case,
then we don't see dontcache doing any good. Even the patched version is
just slightly better compared to buffered case.

But IIUC, dontcache should really shine in cases where we have buffered
writers dirtying the page cache pages which can overflow the RAM size
[1]. The reason why dontcache should show benefit there is, because we
don't see any page cache pressure, since after writeback the pages gets
evicted. Also earlier in the unpatched version, the I/O submission
happens immediately in the same context.

So, I guess, isn't it better to evaluate those scenarios as well with
the patched version - since this series affects those code paths now?

[1]: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20241110152906.1747545-11-axboe@kernel.dk/

---

Thanks!

-ritesh

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a benchmark suite to test the performance of NFSD I/O modes using fio and the libnfs backend against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes tests for buffered, direct, and cached I/O modes via NFSD debugfs controls. It also includes scripts to orchestrate the test matrix, parse results from fio JSON output, and configure the NFS export for testing. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive benchmarking tool for evaluating NFSD performance in various scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE functionality on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine and RWF_DONTCACHE flag. The suite includes various job files for different I/O patterns, such as sequential reads and writes, random reads and writes, and latency measurements. It also includes scripts to run and parse the benchmark results. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive testing framework for IOCB_DONTCACHE on local filesystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag, which prevents caching of written data. The main change is a new scheme that kicks the flusher thread after a DONTCACHE write, causing it to perform writeback proportional to the amount written. This approach was suggested by Jan Kara and Christoph Hellwig during review of an earlier series. The patch also includes benchmark results in the last two patches, which show this method working as well or better than previous approaches.

Correct, but that's sort of the case today too since DONTCACHE I/Os
don't wait for the completion. With this change we're just deferring
the I/O submission to the flusher thread (which should hopefully soon
wake and take care of business). If the flusher thread can't keep up,
then eventually balance_dirty_pages() will kick in and start slowing
things down.

> So is there potential that the system will get all gummed up with
> dirty, to-be-written-soon pagecache?  Is there something which limits
> this buildup?
> 

Today in this situation, the writers are limited by the backing device
throughput. Once the I/O submission queues are full, then the DONTCACHE
writers end up stacking up on those. With this change, the writers will
be more limited by traditional VM limits in this situation. 

In the test runs I did, the peak pagecache with DONTCACHE writes was
higher than with the unpatched version but still considerably less than
with normal buffered I/O. That's the cost of deferring the I/O
submission to the flusher.

One thing we could consider is going back to submitting the writes
inline when the number of dirty pages is high. But, that could have a
detrimental effect on performance too.

game of tradeoffs. The good news is that DONTCACHE is still fairly new

any change here should be rather small.

memory sizes. DONTCACHE gives us a place to experiment with this

the normal buffered case too.

the current blurb about writes:

    cache once the operation completes.

writeback is still just a hint, like it was before. We could mention
about how that I/O can compete with regular buffered I/O, but it seems
a bit like we're adding info that will just be confusing for users.

> Some explaination here of why 5x improvement with NFS compared to local
> filesystems please?
> (I am not much aware of NFS side, but a possible reasoning would help)

I suspect that it's because of the "scattered" nature of nfsd writes.
When the client sends a write to nfsd, we wake a nfsd thread to service
it. So, if there are a lot of writes operating in parallel, they all
get done in the context of different tasks.

My hunch is that this I/O pattern (writing to same file from a bunch of
different threads), particularly suffers from the DONTCACHE inline
write behavior. The threads all end up competing to submit jobs to the
queue and that causes the performance to fall off sharply.

Thanks for the review!

review_comments.raw_body:

hm.  This wasn't what I thought of when I thought of "noisy neighbour".

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Ritesh pointed out that the new writeback path for RWF_DONTCACHE writes may not distinguish between DONTCACHE and non-DONTCACHE dirty pages, potentially leading to delayed writeback of non-dontcache pages in mixed buffered write scenarios. He suggested documenting this change and testing parallel buffered writes and dontcache writes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> writes:

> The IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path in generic_write_sync() calls
> filemap_flush_range() on every write, submitting writeback inline in
> the writer's context.  Perf lock contention profiling shows the
> performance problem is not lock contention but the writeback submission
> work itself — walking the page tree and submitting I/O blocks the writer
> for milliseconds, inflating p99.9 latency from 23ms (buffered) to 93ms
> (dontcache).
>
> Replace the inline filemap_flush_range() call with a flusher kick that
> drains dirty pages in the background.  This moves writeback submission
> completely off the writer's hot path.
>
> To avoid flushing unrelated buffered dirty data, add a dedicated
> WB_start_dontcache bit and wb_check_start_dontcache() handler that uses
> the new NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY counter to determine how many pages to write
> back.  The flusher writes back that many pages from the oldest dirty
> inodes (not restricted to dontcache-specific inodes). This helps
> preserve I/O batching while limiting the scope of expedited writeback.
>

Yup, so, we wakeup the writeback flusher, which will write those many
"number" of dirty pages. Those dirty pages written by writeback, can be
of any type though, can be DONTCACHE or normal (non-dontcache) dirty
pages. IIUC, writeback doesn't distinguish between them while writing.


IMO, what we could also include in the commit msg is why is this above
approach taken? IIUC, that is because, by writing NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY
pages, it still reduces the page cache pressure and still reduces the
amount of work that the reclaim has to do, even though some of those
pages maybe non-dontcache pages, in case if there was a parallel
buffered write in the system.


Also should the following change be documented somewhere? Like in Man
page maybe? i.e.
Earlier RWF_DONTCACHE writes made sure that those dirty pages are
immediately submitted for writeback and completion would release those
pages. But now, in certain cases when there is a mixed buffered write in
the system, those dontcache dirty pages might be written back after a
delay (whenever the next time writeback kicks in).
However for RWF_DONTCACHE reads, it should not affect anything.

> Like WB_start_all, the WB_start_dontcache bit coalesces multiple
> DONTCACHE writes into a single flusher wakeup without per-write
> allocations.
>
> Also add WB_REASON_DONTCACHE as a new writeback reason for tracing
> visibility, and target the correct cgroup writeback domain via

>

Since this patch series definitely affects that.

Can you please help describe this test scenario if possible.. In above
you mentioned we are writing file_size as 2x RAM_SIZE. But your

local num_clients=4

Also the multi-writer case is spawning parallel fio jobs, and then

> NFS engine with libnfs, 1024 NFSD threads, pool_mode=pernode,

The reason I am thinking of this is: dontcache marked pages, gets
evicted from page cache after they are written back. But this patch
series can now delay that from happening when there is a parallel
buffered writer dirtying page cache pages. Because of the reasons we
already discussed...

Note that, this may not be a workload which matters in the real world,
but I was thinking, it will be good to know the impact if any, of such
workload with this patch series (parallel buffered and dontcache

I guess you missed answering this. The reason why I was asking about this is....

>> >                        baseline    patched     change
>> >   buffered              1619.5     1611.2      -0.5%
>> >   dontcache             1281.1     1629.4     +27.2%
>> >   direct                1545.4     1609.4      +4.1%
>> > 

... If we see the performace of buffered and dontcache in baseline case,
then we don't see dontcache doing any good. Even the patched version is
just slightly better compared to buffered case.

But IIUC, dontcache should really shine in cases where we have buffered
writers dirtying the page cache pages which can overflow the RAM size
[1]. The reason why dontcache should show benefit there is, because we
don't see any page cache pressure, since after writeback the pages gets
evicted. Also earlier in the unpatched version, the I/O submission
happens immediately in the same context.

So, I guess, isn't it better to evaluate those scenarios as well with
the patched version - since this series affects those code paths now?

[1]: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20241110152906.1747545-11-axboe@kernel.dk/

>> 
>> Nice :)
>> Some explaination here of why 5x improvement with NFS compared to local
>> filesystems please?
>> (I am not much aware of NFS side, but a possible reasoning would help)
>> 
>
> I suspect that it's because of the "scattered" nature of nfsd writes.
> When the client sends a write to nfsd, we wake a nfsd thread to service
> it. So, if there are a lot of writes operating in parallel, they all
> get done in the context of different tasks.
>
> My hunch is that this I/O pattern (writing to same file from a bunch of
> different threads), particularly suffers from the DONTCACHE inline
> write behavior. The threads all end up competing to submit jobs to the
> queue and that causes the performance to fall off sharply.
>

Thanks!

-ritesh

One comment regarding how the writeback is started:

> +static long wb_check_start_dontcache(struct bdi_writeback *wb)
> +{
> +	long nr_pages;
> +
> +	if (!test_bit(WB_start_dontcache, &wb->state))
> +		return 0;
> +
> +	nr_pages = global_node_page_state(NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY);
> +	if (nr_pages) {
> +		struct wb_writeback_work work = {
> +			.nr_pages	= wb_split_bdi_pages(wb, nr_pages),
> +			.sync_mode	= WB_SYNC_NONE,
> +			.range_cyclic	= 1,
> +			.reason		= WB_REASON_DONTCACHE,
> +		};
> +
> +		nr_pages = wb_writeback(wb, &work);
> +	}
> +
> +	clear_bit(WB_start_dontcache, &wb->state);
> +	return nr_pages;
> +}

So this will end up splitting NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY folios among per-cgroup wb
structures based on their writeback bandwidth. This is a reasonable thing
for global writeback where the bandwidth more or less corresponds to the
amount of dirty folios. However with DONTCACHE I expect big differences in
among NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY among different cgroups not necessarily
corresponding to wb throughput. In particular if you do DONTCACHE writes
from one cgroup and normal writes from another cgroup this will
systematically underestimate the amount needed to write by a factor of
about two.

So I think the stat should be a per bdi_writeback one (instead of a per node
one) which would avoid the need to split the value to wb here.

								Honza


-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking
Re: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch modifies the behavior of writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE operations, which are used to prevent caching of writes. The change ensures that writeback is kicked off for these operations, similar to calling sync_file_range(2) with SYNC_FILE_RANGE_WRITE. This is a hint and does not provide hard guarantees on the state of the page cache. The patch addresses performance issues caused by competing I/O patterns, particularly when multiple threads write to the same file in parallel.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
Ritesh noted that the patch series can delay eviction of don't cache marked pages due to parallel buffered writers dirtying page cache pages, which may impact performance in certain workloads.

Reviewer noted that the performance benefits of IOCB_DONTCACHE are not evident in baseline cases, and suggested evaluating its impact on scenarios where buffered writers dirty page cache pages that can overflow RAM size, as this is where dontcache should shine due to reduced page cache pressure.

Reviewer Ritesh noted that the patch's change to kick off writeback for IOCB_DONTCACHE may cause performance issues due to competition between parallel and buffered writes, particularly in nfsd's scattered I/O pattern.
review_comments.raw_body:
The reason I am thinking of this is: dontcache marked pages, gets
evicted from page cache after they are written back. But this patch
series can now delay that from happening when there is a parallel
buffered writer dirtying page cache pages. Because of the reasons we
already discussed...

Note that, this may not be a workload which matters in the real world,
but I was thinking, it will be good to know the impact if any, of such
workload with this patch series (parallel buffered and dontcache
writers).

---

... If we see the performace of buffered and dontcache in baseline case,
then we don't see dontcache doing any good. Even the patched version is
just slightly better compared to buffered case.

But IIUC, dontcache should really shine in cases where we have buffered
writers dirtying the page cache pages which can overflow the RAM size
[1]. The reason why dontcache should show benefit there is, because we
don't see any page cache pressure, since after writeback the pages gets
evicted. Also earlier in the unpatched version, the I/O submission
happens immediately in the same context.

So, I guess, isn't it better to evaluate those scenarios as well with
the patched version - since this series affects those code paths now?

[1]: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20241110152906.1747545-11-axboe@kernel.dk/

---

Thanks!

-ritesh

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 5/8] fuse: {io-uring} Allow reduced number of ring queues
Re: [PATCH v4 5/8] fuse: {io-uring} Allow reduced number of ring queues
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch allows FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) servers to use registered buffers/zero copy with a reduced number of ring queues, which is less than the total number of CPUs on the machine. This change addresses a request from server administrators who want to optimize memory allocation and usage. The approach taken is to relax the constraint on the number of ring queues required for zero-copy operations.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that even if buffers are associated with a specific queue, the current patch still allows for dynamic registration of queues and suggested this should also be possible with pBuf
review_comments.raw_body:
If the buffer is associated with queue, queues still can be registered
dynamically? I don't say that your should be forced to do dynamic
queues, it is just that the current reduced queue patches allow to have
that. And with pBuf I don't see why it shouldn't work either.

Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author initially misunderstood the patch as addressing dynamic queues instead of registered buffers, but after re-reading it, confirmed that it would be compatible with registered buffers and suggested a possible adaptation for dynamic queues.
review_comments.raw_body:
I got the buffer pinning mixed up with dynamic queue ents, not dynamic
queues. I reread your patch and it would be compatible with the
registered buffers interface. Dyanmic queues would work with
group_cpus_evenly() as well I believe, we would just need to rebuild
the cpu-to-qid mapping for each new queue, but this seems ok since
it's a fixed registration cost.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/14] mm: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING by introducing a new configuration option, CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE. This allows architectures that support present-but-inaccessible page table entries (protnone PTEs) to select this option independently of NUMA balancing. The six supported architectures are updated to select this option, and the protnone infrastructure is made available for use beyond NUMA balancing, such as in userfaultfd working set tracking. No functional changes are introduced, but the infrastructure is now decoupled from NUMA balancing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/14] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE bit macros to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the uffd-wp PTE bit macros to simply 'uffd' across various architectures, including x86 and arm64. The change is purely mechanical and does not alter any behavior. The rename reflects that the bit now tracks userfaultfd access rather than just write protection or NUMA hinting. This patch is part of a larger series of changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/14] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE accessors to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames kernel-internal names related to the uffd-wp PTE bit, which is reused for marking access-tracking PTEs alongside write-protected ones. The rename affects various header files and source code locations across different architectures. The changes are purely mechanical, with no behavior change intended. This is part of a larger patch series that aims to update userfaultfd functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/14] mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag, VM_UFFD_RWP, to enable userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP). RWP extends the existing write-protection mechanism to also trap read accesses through userfaultfd. The flag is only meaningful when both PAGE_NONE and uffd PTE bit primitives exist. A Kconfig symbol, CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP, is introduced to auto-select this feature when certain conditions are met. The patch includes changes to various kernel files to add support for the new flag and its associated functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/14] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new flags to the change_protection() function: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE, which are used for userfaultfd (UFFD) read-write protection. The MM_CP_UFFD_RWP flag installs a PAGE_NONE PTE with the uffd bit set, while the MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE flag restores the original vma->vm_page_prot and clears the uffd bit. These flags are used to ensure that UFFD RWP is handled consistently across different memory types, including anonymous, shared memory, transparent huge pages, and hugetlb. The patch also updates several functions in the mm module to use these new flags.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/14] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that the RWP (Read-Only Write-Protection) marker is preserved across PTE rewrites in various kernel paths, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect operations. Without this preservation, the RWP tracking could be silently dropped, leading to unexpected behavior when accessing pages with PAGE_NONE semantics. The patch updates several functions to correctly handle the uffd bit and VM_UFFD_RWP flag in these scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/14] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends three mm paths outside the fault handler gate to treat VM_UFFD_RWP (User Faultfd Read-Write Protected) the same as VM_UFFD_WP (User Faultfd Write Protected). The affected paths are khugepaged, rmap, and GUP. In khugepaged, ranges carrying the uffd PTE bit are now protected from collapse. In rmap, unmap batching is capped at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP to ensure each PTE is handled individually. In GUP, faults are forced through the userfaultfd fault path regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT. This ensures that per-PTE RWP state is not silently destroyed or bypassed.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/14] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT plumbing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd interface, allowing userspace to track read-write protection (RWP) on registered memory ranges. The feature includes two new ioctls: UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT, which enable RWP tracking and allow installing or removing RWP on a range. The patch also introduces a capability bit, UFFD_FEATURE_RWP, to advertise the feature. The implementation ensures that re-registering a range does not drop existing per-PTE markers, preventing stale markers from causing write-faults. The feature is not yet advertised and will be exposed in a follow-up patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/14] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds read-write protection (RWP) fault delivery to the userfaultfd mechanism, allowing userspace to track and resolve RWP faults. It introduces new functions to handle RWP faults at different memory hierarchy levels and exposes a new UAPI to register for RWP fault notifications. The patch also updates various kernel files to support RWP faults in anonymous, shmem, and hugetlb memory types. The approach taken is to wire the fault side of RWP tracking and provide userspace with a way to resolve RWP faults using the userfaultfd interface.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/14] mm/pagemap: add PAGE_IS_ACCESSED for RWP tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new category, PAGE_IS_ACCESSED, to the pagemap API for tracking read-write protection (RWP) using userfaultfd. It introduces a separate PTE bit for RWP and scopes it to the VMA that registered the mode making the bit meaningful. The patch also updates documentation and code to reflect this change, ensuring that the UAPI does not conflate write-protected and RWP-protected pages.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 11/14] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd system called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC, which allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write-protected (RWP) faults. This is useful for working-set tracking in virtual machines, where the kernel can restore access to pages without blocking the faulting thread. The VMM can then reconstruct the access pattern after the fact using PAGEMAP_SCAN. Additionally, this patch upgrades writable private anonymous PTEs when necessary, preventing COW-faults on re-access of RWP'd writable pages.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 12/14] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl, UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which allows a VMM to toggle between sync and async RWP modes at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC being toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This feature is useful for VMMs that need to switch between sync and async modes on-the-fly, such as during working set scanning or page eviction. The patch also includes changes to ensure that fault-path readers of ctx->features are excluded from seeing intermediate states during the toggle operation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 13/14] selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds userfaultfd (UFFD) read-write-protect (RWP) tests to the Linux kernel selftest suite. The tests cover various scenarios, including asynchronous and synchronous RWP modes, page mapping, memory protection, and fork handling. The tests verify that UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT work correctly in different situations. The patch includes 11 test cases that run against anonymous, shared memory, and hugetlb memory types.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 14/14] Documentation/userfaultfd: document RWP working set tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature, specifically covering read-write protection (RWP) working set tracking. It explains how RWP works, including its sync and async fault models, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT semantics, and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. The documentation also covers a typical workflow for virtual machine (VMM) working-set-tracking, from detection to eviction and back to asynchronous mode.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for tracking the working set of VM guest memory using userfaultfd, enabling a VMM to identify cold pages and evict them to tiered or remote storage. The approach involves introducing a new userfaultfd registration mode (RWP) that uses the same mechanism as existing modes but with additional functionality.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer provided a detailed explanation of the problem and approach taken in the patch series. They highlighted the benefits of using userfaultfd for working set tracking and described the new registration mode (RWP) and its functionality.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: The reviewer asked for clarification on the interaction between RWP and existing modes, specifically how re-registering a range would affect per-PTE markers.

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: The reviewer suggested that the patch series should be split into smaller, more manageable pieces to facilitate review and discussion.
